districts: Central Valley is the correct district name.

featuredQuotes.text: I think there's a lot of research showing that computers of any kind are a distraction in class and lead to cognitive overload. I'm a college professor and anecdotally I see major deficits in attention, critical and creative thinking, as well as basic social interaction. I ban all devices in my own classes for this reason.

my child's behavior and focus are directly impacted by screen time. Screens are addicting and pushing it instead of book for education does more harm than good in elementary schools. All screens should be removed from elementary schools. Patents should not have to pay 30k a year to send their kid to a waldorf school to not have the tech. This is crazy

I think there needs to be better alignment of children's need to explore, be creative, regulate stress, stay grounded, and engage in a traditional social setting.  Technology clearly eliminates social aspects, increases serotonin levels and creates behavioral challenges and addictive nature.

These devices are a nightmare for kids with issues with attention span/executive function deficits. They are a massive source of distraction in school and out of school. Some kids are absolutely addicted to screens, and we should not be feeding that addiction at school.

There is extensive research to show that increased screen time is detrimental to children. The use of technology is impacting attention, emotional regulation, mental health capacity, real life dysphoria, and impacting our children’s ability to learn.

I’m very concerned about the lack of evidence of growth using ed tech vs “old school methods”. The data suggests their is no growth using the ed tech and could argue it is creating a disconnect between student and teacher/classroom. The more we rely on AI and Ed Tech, the more detrimental to the development of our children.

The large Smart Screen covers the whiteboard area, and when it's on, I see that it dominates the attention of the young children. Often during drop off in the morning there is a song playing on Youtube - with kids focused on the screen as they dance (or not). They often watch movies during rest time after lunch.

The school trys to put restritions on the computer so the kids can't play games at school, on the bus and at home but the students always find away around it.  I have ways of regulating their screen time on the devices I give them, but it's impossible to regulate it well when they have they school issued device at home all the time.

Bucks.text: I think there's a lot of research showing that computers of any kind are a distraction in class and lead to cognitive overload. I'm a college professor and anecdotally I see major deficits in attention, critical and creative thinking, as well as basic social interaction. I ban all devices in my own classes for this reason.

my child's behavior and focus are directly impacted by screen time. Screens are addicting and pushing it instead of book for education does more harm than good in elementary schools. All screens should be removed from elementary schools. Patents should not have to pay 30k a year to send their kid to a waldorf school to not have the tech. This is crazy

As a middle school teacher, I feel like screens are a giant problem at that level or with that group of students. They are addicted. I primarily do not use screens in my class bc when using them, students are often using them inappropriately and off task. When in study hall, they just game instead of doing actual work.

I believe it's a slipperly slope and hard to do minimal use.  Meaning I am essentially ok with the minimal screen time they have (although I do think my 2nd grader has slightly too much of it), however I believe they just shouldn't use it in school in general.  Even in small amounts, I wish it was banned altogether.

Devices given at school should stay at school.  Separately, how does a school district distribute devices that have no controls and limitations?  I got an email from my 4th graders teacher about things they were looking up.  I don’t blame him, I blame the school for having them use these with no parental type controls in place.

I don’t believe any screen time is necessary or beneficial to students at the elementary grade levels. This should begin at middle school with vey strict and teacher guided policies that are limited and within certain class times. More like a computer class and everything else done as we used to with paper books.

Montgomery.text: I think there needs to be better alignment of children's need to explore, be creative, regulate stress, stay grounded, and engage in a traditional social setting.  Technology clearly eliminates social aspects, increases serotonin levels and creates behavioral challenges and addictive nature.

There is extensive research to show that increased screen time is detrimental to children. The use of technology is impacting attention, emotional regulation, mental health capacity, real life dysphoria, and impacting our children’s ability to learn.

I’m very concerned about the lack of evidence of growth using ed tech vs “old school methods”. The data suggests their is no growth using the ed tech and could argue it is creating a disconnect between student and teacher/classroom. The more we rely on AI and Ed Tech, the more detrimental to the development of our children.

The school trys to put restritions on the computer so the kids can't play games at school, on the bus and at home but the students always find away around it.  I have ways of regulating their screen time on the devices I give them, but it's impossible to regulate it well when they have they school issued device at home all the time.

My understanding is that next year (3rd grade) the Chromebooks start coming home and usage really ramps up. This is entirely unacceptable to me. I don’t mind its limited use as one of many tools in school, but I think inviting screens in to a greater extent opens up all of the issues above, plus the potential for cyber bullying.

Wissahickon SD ended its 1:1 device policy for K & 1st in the fall of 2024. My older child watched entire episodes of PBS Kids cartoons or played games every day all through kindergarten. Eliminating the 1:1 policy made such a difference for my younger child's experience.

Allegheny.text: These devices are a nightmare for kids with issues with attention span/executive function deficits. They are a massive source of distraction in school and out of school. Some kids are absolutely addicted to screens, and we should not be feeding that addiction at school.

All of it. The schools that are implementing this stuff are not capable of keeping up with technology and technology use, and are requiring children to be better than adaults by not being distracted or avoiding their real work. Like they want to complain about the kids behavior, but pps created this problem.

My daughter really dislikes when everyone is on their screen during recess. Thankfully this has decreased with new rules. I am concerned about whether generative AI tools will be used as I do not trust them to always be accurate or appropriate. I wish we would have whitelisted sites instead of blacklisting some sites as there will always be new websites.

I am concerned about required usage for "educational" apps that school districts make for our students, which then require them to use apps a certain amount regardless of their effectiveness. Selling our students' screen time to app developers and technology companies is completely inappropriate.

Have had multiple instances with both children being exposed to inappropriate/ mature content on their school devices, despite school filters. Both of my children have ADHD and screens exacerbate that. My oldest also uses their school device as an 'escape' during class when they are overwhelmed.

For kids with learning disabilities devices can create larger hurdles as expectations assignments, etc. become more ambiguous and the distraction of playing games and streaming videos is at their fingertips and they do not have the impulse control to stay focused on school tasks and assignments.

Delaware.text: The large Smart Screen covers the whiteboard area, and when it's on, I see that it dominates the attention of the young children. Often during drop off in the morning there is a song playing on Youtube - with kids focused on the screen as they dance (or not). They often watch movies during rest time after lunch.

In one of my kids classes the teacher doesn't lecture, he tells them to move through the screen by screen learning modules on the chrome books in the classroom. I feel strongly that this is removing the opportunity for kids to listen to a lecture. This is very sad to me. I am also very upset about the constant every day, all day, updates of their grades.

We need all devices out of our schools, save for a few select cases. The research is clear that all devices (laptops, phones, etc.) hinder student learning, focus, and engagement. Technology also represents a large spend, which is an issue in out district.

My son talks about videos he’s watching at school about Minecraft and also says he has access to YouTube.  These platforms are inappropriate for school.  This is ridiculous and unacceptable.  The teachers default to screens way way too often.  If the teachers need a break, give the kids more recess time, not screen time.

The district’s reliance on devices is misguided. Routine screen use is not synonymous with academic rigor or future readiness. Students do not need constant exposure to technology to succeed in a digital world — they need strong literacy, critical thinking, attention, and interpersonal skills.

Kids have plenty of opportunity to interact with screens outside of school, and I hate to see kids dedicating to screens the school hours that could be spent interacting with peers and staff, creating hands on work, extra recess, or diving into extracurricular topics within the classroom.

Chester.text: I would like screens banned in elementary school ideally but at minimum a return to shared bank that they visit on a rotation. I think it's terrible that my kids use screens more than they have gym in a week.  We can learn typing and concepts of coding etc without  damaging devices and gym should be every day.

I am disappointed that writing assignments are being completed on an ipad from start to finish.  I think they are missing an important learning experience from editing their paper and each other's papers before typing a final draft, since they are unable to print a rough draft to look over.

In our three years of 1:1 devices, I have not seen one project or use of the technology that has impressed me. I also think it's important to note how these devices are being used to "show" work back to parents, sometimes the kids themselves are uploading and sharing their work. It just feels like busy work to do all this sharing.

Data privacy, reinforcing addictive product design (gamification), location tracking, and normalization of personal device use at home at way too young of an age. There needs to be an opt-out option for elementary school at a minimum.

There is little to no research that states any benefits for kids being on technology, especially as it relates to their learning. It’s also difficult to actually monitor what kids are doing and how, even with filters and such.
In particular, attention span, critical thinking, writing.  I'd prefer pencil, paper, textbooks.  100%.  Type the final version, some online research would be okay with me.

Luzerne.text: We were not given an option of wanting a device. We are also responsible for any damages that may occur to said device throughout the year. Students also bring device home throughout the summer further undermining our strict no device policy at home.

Lackawanna.text: My son’s teacher told us that there is no way for the school to block everything inappropriate. As parents, I feel as if the school is undoing a lot of our hard work when it comes to limiting screen time and ensuring we know what they are accessing.

Philadelphia.text: The children are always trying to figure out ways to "jailbreak" their chromebooks to access restricted sites, or just finding new sites that aren't restricted yet. My child doesn't use the school-issued chromebook at home, but has to carry it around to and from school everyday because there is "no place to store it".

The School District of Philadelphia issue chromebooks to families during COVID but then kept them in place afterwards without asking families if we wanted to take that on. We're now responsible for these devices and we don't want to be. Additionally, the protections aren't great and parents can't put controls on them.

I'm deeply against the use of generative AI in schools in any grade. I support the consideration of and research into the benefits of analytical AI for teachers, for certain projects, etc, but think generative AI use runs counter to the types of cognitive skills I most want my kids to develop.

Screen time replaces teacher led interactions. For example, the teacher doesn't do story time on the rug in my child's Kindergarten class. Instead, a book is played on YouTube on the smart board instead. Although both are stories, I feel like the YouTube story is unnecessary.
We are never told what the screen policy is or how much they are using. I’m reliant on my 7th to tell me how she played “x” math game all afternoon

My biggest concern is school devices undermining screen time policies at home. My 5th grader exclusively has homework on her Chromebook; it’s difficult to police where homework ends and YouTube time begins.

Centre.text: We are a family with very limited screen time at home. I am not pleased that the State College Area School District, a district in a highly-educated area surrounded with research PhDs, allows so much screen time in the schools.

I don't think early elementary school kids belong on devices in school beyond maybe a once per week computer lab for exposure. Hands on learning is more important and they cannot read and are still working in letter recognition so computers are not needed.

I do not think that kindergarteners should have computers or personal screen time AT ALL. Starting later (even third or fourth grade) would be better. I have looked at the "educational" programs my children use at school, and most, especially the reading apps, have no educational value at all in my opinion.

Berks.text: Thank you very much for all of your work. I am a mother and as a Doctor of Physical Therapy with advanced training in pediatrics and 8 years of experience reading the science on the effects of the radiation used by cell phones and other wireless devices, I have a special appreciation for what you are doing.
It's difficult to help them study when everything is electronic. I never see them read books and don't think electronic texts help them learn with depth.
The kids need to have more time playing and running less time with white boards tvs and chromebooks in schools. Every school lesson is an app even religion. It's sad.
Research shows that information is better retained when students used physical books and worksheets.
I am a substitute teacher in my child's school district and have seen firsthand how impossible it is to get kids to stay on task when 1:1 devices are used

Lycoming.text:
The older the grade the more they're seen on their devices.

I also believe that Keyboarding should be more of a priority in the elementary grades. If students are exposed/required to become so dependent on using the technology, why are they be supported in how to use it appropriately and develop a lifelong skill.

Platforms to access coursework is cumbersome and frustrating to some students; remote learning days are worksheets in digital format…no interaction with teacher or immediate feedback to check for understanding. Students are connected to a device to cover the material in many classes when weather days prevent them from attending in person.

I would like more information communicated to families about the actual use of the iPads during school, such as the apps and programs that are being used, the average amount of screen time per day, etc.
Just what’s already been said. We need to get back to using books and putting pencil to paper. Have a classes with allotted time in a computer lab if technology is needed.
Early language is essential to learning to read. Parents and students do not communicate anymore. Families "sit" on their devices. The addiction is real
It’s more difficult for me, as a parent, to know and see what my child is learning, and it is more difficult for me to help him study for tests.

Cumberland.text: I genuinely have no idea how much time my daughter is in her iPad every day. The school doesn’t communicate it well. We used to do screens on a regular basis at home but we have stopped because of how much ambiguity there is with her school usage.

Westmoreland.text: I absolutely hate screens and the negative effects it has on my ADHD child. I wish there was an option to opt out.  Science shows how detrimental screens are to children yet we continue to rely on them.  It makes nonsense.  It is unfair to the families who are trying to be screen free.
These iPad usages weather or not they use a little or a lot is just too much. They are causing more issues with learning and social factors. I would rather my child use pencil and paper.

My 6 year old son in kindergarten told me today that “most of his friends watch YouTube on the school iPad” 
6… years… old… in kindergarten.

Lancaster.text: Our school district allows YouTube, which is not something our children have access to at home. Our district also uses Aristotle, however it works haphazardly. This past fall, my 12 year old came across and Ai chat site through his school issued Chromebook.
My younger children have never even used an iPad or tablet or computer, then they’re expected to use devices at 5-6 years old. Let them be kids and explore the world through senses, not screens.

Beaver.text: Most of our major conflicts in our home with our younger kids have been around ipad use at home and on the bus. In fact, I've begun to take my 3rd schooler to and from school just to alleviate 30 minutes each way of extra ipad use on games, youtube, etc.
I am afraid of how the issues listed above are overall harming my child's cognitive development.

Erie.text: Data privacy is my only concern. Use of third-party services are so tempting for schools that do not have the budgets or skills to build the technology they want to use or teach in-house.
I'm working with a group of moms to get a bell to bell ban at the high school. It's progressing but it's already behind others.
I think that there is such an emphasis on screens rather than hands on learning. It is concerning the eye strain it gives children as well.

Monroe.text: Children are not taught how to read/write/type before being given chromebooks to use for school assignments.   Kindergarteners should not have Chromebooks at all!

Carbon.text: Ignorance of how screens impact learning (teachers not given a choice and also not provided information on how to utilize technology without negatively impacting learning)

Lebanon.text: Some grade levels are allowed to have their screens at lunch, which is of major concern to me.

Lehigh.text: Chromebooks should stay in School Carts during the week and taken out when needed.

Palisades.text: I think there's a lot of research showing that computers of any kind are a distraction in class and lead to cognitive overload. I'm a college professor and anecdotally I see major deficits in attention, critical and creative thinking, as well as basic social interaction. I ban all devices in my own classes for this reason.
This entire strategy of screen usage needs to be rolled back to prioritize social and physical development.

Central Bucks.text: my child's behavior and focus are directly impacted by screen time. Screens are addicting and pushing it instead of book for education does more harm than good in elementary schools. All screens should be removed from elementary schools. Patents should not have to pay 30k a year to send their kid to a waldorf school to not have the tech. This is crazy

As a middle school teacher, I feel like screens are a giant problem at that level or with that group of students. They are addicted. I primarily do not use screens in my class bc when using them, students are often using them inappropriately and off task. When in study hall, they just game instead of doing actual work.

I believe it's a slipperly slope and hard to do minimal use.  Meaning I am essentially ok with the minimal screen time they have (although I do think my 2nd grader has slightly too much of it), however I believe they just shouldn't use it in school in general.  Even in small amounts, I wish it was banned altogether.

Devices given at school should stay at school.  Separately, how does a school district distribute devices that have no controls and limitations?  I got an email from my 4th graders teacher about things they were looking up.  I don’t blame him, I blame the school for having them use these with no parental type controls in place.

I don’t believe any screen time is necessary or beneficial to students at the elementary grade levels. This should begin at middle school with vey strict and teacher guided policies that are limited and within certain class times. More like a computer class and everything else done as we used to with paper books.

Device use is rampant at school, a constant distraction. It’s like putting a plate of donuts in front of a person on a diet. You are asking too much for these kids to control inpulses for you tube, search and games. Some teachers are putting all assignments on there.

It’s really difficult to understand how much time is being spent on screens. It absolutely in my observation while being a PT teacher aid elementary school I was at is being used as a reward or sometimes a pacifier for certain students. I know and want our children to be taught how to have a healthy relationship with technology.

Despite district filters and automated device usage reports sent to me, my 8th grader spends a huge amount of time playing games, watching YouTube shorts, and checking professional sports statistics while at school. When he comes home, I spend time sitting with him helping him stay on track while he gets his online school work completed.

There doesn’t seem to be an understanding at board level that Ed Tech is harmful. I have tried to raise these concerns and was told they had great success with these programs, but I  feel they need to reconsider how they define success.

While I like that they have individual devices, I wish they could leave at school.  Is this even an option?  We have started to take them away at home, after any needed homework is completed, so that they are not continually distracted by a device

I think devices should remain at school for 3-6 as well. Any homework can be paper and pencil. I also don’t think the majority of students should have access to technology during indoor recess, arrival, or dismissal time (except for students with IEPs)
They can get away with way more at school than at home. It is very frustrating that they have no real protection from things that harm their minds.
With all the data that exists about the effects of screens on children, I have been unpleasantly surprised by the prevalence of iPads and other screens in my child's first grade class.
The policies on bringing the device has changed through the years. Regardless, having a child responsible for the back and forth transport of the device is another problematic layer.

Strong dependency on devices for communication and updates. Use of phones and apps also required to receive communication.

Without app access- family isn’t able to intercept assignments and teacher communication
Teachers use videos on YouTube as a learning tool or to create a break when the kids are misbehaving

Hatboro-Horsham.text: I think there needs to be better alignment of children's need to explore, be creative, regulate stress, stay grounded, and engage in a traditional social setting.  Technology clearly eliminates social aspects, increases serotonin levels and creates behavioral challenges and addictive nature.

Upper St. Clair.text: These devices are a nightmare for kids with issues with attention span/executive function deficits. They are a massive source of distraction in school and out of school. Some kids are absolutely addicted to screens, and we should not be feeding that addiction at school.

Kids bringing home devices so it's one more device at home to have to police...at least I definitely don't think sending the device home is necessary in Elementary.  I know they want it charged but then I've found my son discovering how to use it for games and chatting with his friends when I don't even allow that on our home devices.
bullying and being introduced to things too early.  For example, all the young girls on the Sephora trend, many get allergies from the products and they are too young to fully understand the impace.
I'd rather the schools use their edtech budgets to pay teachers a fair wage and provide more enrichment opportunities for students.
My 2nd grader figured out how to chat with a friend through a shared Google document.
Learning is encouraged through the use of games which become addictive
At one point we heard that screens were sometimes used during indoor recess.
EMF-so many devices and all connected to wi-fi throughout the buildings.

Springfield Township.text: There is extensive research to show that increased screen time is detrimental to children. The use of technology is impacting attention, emotional regulation, mental health capacity, real life dysphoria, and impacting our children’s ability to learn.

My 4th grader is not performing well in school and her online test taking appears to be much worse than pencil and paper. Having questions read aloud is distracting, and the temptation to just click on a button to select an answer and move on makes it easy for her to speed through the test without really thinking.
Parents feel like they have no voice. I lead several software development teams, I'm very close to forcing software where it doesn't belong. We're trading convenience for literally nothing here.
I also find the value of educational games and apps to be quite suspect in the lower grades.
Some teachers let the kids go on their phones in high school if they have extra time
Kids can’t have face to face conversations and don’t understand facial reactions

Colonial.text: I’m very concerned about the lack of evidence of growth using ed tech vs “old school methods”. The data suggests their is no growth using the ed tech and could argue it is creating a disconnect between student and teacher/classroom. The more we rely on AI and Ed Tech, the more detrimental to the development of our children.

I really hate the use of the Google classroom feature where individual's submitted work is viewable by all students in the classroom. It leads to judgement, criticism, and bullying of students based on both the content of their answers and their proficiency at proper grammar. The use of YouTube in instruction is also way overused.

My 3rd grader comes home and wants to play games that seem educational that he plays at school but they have little, if any, educational value. For example, blooket seems to be a lot of clicking the mouse to “hack” or create “distractions” for other players. It also included questionable pop song lyrics as trivia.

I am a teacher of upper grades at a different school (independent), so I know that finding a way to have kids work independently while I do one-on-one instruction with struggling students is clutch.  I do think, though, that there are apps being used when kids could be working on screen.

We have tried to limit screen time at home, but since covid it seems the schools rely heavily on screens. It's impossible to limit when my children need devices for classwork. Most of the time they are just scrolling Youtube. I would prefer if devices stayed at school.

My kindergartner has complained about screen time in school, saying that he’s supposed to only use screens at homes. The “Brain breaks” are just YouTube videos, which is wild to me for this age range, even if they make the kids get up and move around. Why not play Simon Says with a bunch of kindergartners?

My other concern is the testing that’s done on computers. Is this in service of PSSA readiness or is there some sort of benefit to this? It makes it very hard for me to understand the scores of the test. It’s hard to decipher if they were dumb mistakes or if it’s not understand the concept.

Concerns about computers being used during indoor recess.

  I’ve heard of kids being on games they weren’t supposed to but the teacher doesn’t know because they are in front of the classroom. 

I’m not even sure HOW MUCH time is being used on computers because that isn’t shared.

My children are now older, and my youngest is a senior. As an educator myself. I can see how reliant the children are on their devices. I think it's very concerning at the Elementary level how much time some of the students are spending on their devices.

There are not enough filters and my child can easily access other websites during class or during homework.  For homework, even a 6th grader needs constant monitoring to ensure that they do not get off task and start looking at others sites.
I think less screens overall are better for kids. I know they’re argument of it’s augmenting learning, but it can be augmented in less harmful ways than screens.
Currently enrolled my child for K next year and I am very concerned about the use of tech in schools. I am a BCBA and I have clients in other districts.
My child often tells me that they use the computer in music or library when that seems to be two places that absolutely do not need screens.  I would prefer no screens until 3rd grade or older.
I believe that screens are not as effective of a way to learn as more traditional models, and it is showing in the data of children not doing as well academically in this generation.
Electronics impair my child’s ability to be creative, play, and have internal emotional regulation due to the instant gratification that devices provide.
Devices are used during indoor recess. The kids should be interacting. Devices are being used for tests. Is that too much for a child?
I think as kids get older, exposure to computers makes sense. No child before age 8 really needs screen time in schools.
I have no idea how much screentime my kids are getting, but I know they use screens to learn some reading and math.
Kindergarden is too young. Should be older age where we introduce Chromebook. K gets chromebooks!
Both of my children were exposed to pornographic content while using chrome books at Ridge Park.
My daughter uses her device for indoor recess. I’d much rather her interact with her peers.

Lower Merion.text: The school trys to put restritions on the computer so the kids can't play games at school, on the bus and at home but the students always find away around it.  I have ways of regulating their screen time on the devices I give them, but it's impossible to regulate it well when they have they school issued device at home all the time.

This seems like common sense that everyone should be on board with. I feel people are concerned children will be left behind technologically but i cannot see that happening when the industry and models are changing so rapidly- it will be different every year.

For middle school laptops are particularly troublesome in school because the students watch YouTube videos in class, play games on the laptops are a distraction to instruction. At home, it’s especially problematic because our child takes a laptop in to her room play Roblox, chats and emails friends when she’s supposed to be doing homework.

It is ruining childhood and making them less smart. They stay too much, they use it for hours when they get home and act like it’s required. I have no way of regulating what they do for supposed school related activities. I get that it was convenient for pandemic learning but it is such a lazy decision to continue it now.

I am not anti-technology. I am really just wanting my child to learn the actual concepts in the classroom and at school. I think if we have technology supporting the learning that’s OK but when they are just independently trying to surf through different games and activities they are not really learning.

Inconsistencies across the district regarding classroom screen usage.  I appreciate the teachers who minimize screen time in their classrooms, even though that goes against what they are told to do by upper administration.

Also, lack of physical textbooks and novels - starting in 5th grade (middle school) almost everything seems to be on the screen.

There is no information shared with parents about which apps are used. There is no information shared with parents how many minutes a day children are on screens. There doesn't seem to be a way to opt out of 1:1 devices because everything is on Google classroom, Schoology, etc.

I would like to know if there is a clear law that parents can opt out of ed tech and request paper-pen approach for our kid. If such a law exists, how can I ask for it and make sure to get it without any pushback from the school?

Over reliance, lack of hands on material makes it practically impossible for parents to help child with homework or assignments, as we don’t have access to the text/lessons to review what is being taught.
Having the computer at home creates more work for us in limiting their screen time as we can lock a school device.
I would prefer to keep screens out of the classroom and work entirely with paper and pencil with the small exception of learning to type
Not being able to provide or reject parental consent for use of/data collection by individual "educational" apps

Upper Dublin.text: My understanding is that next year (3rd grade) the Chromebooks start coming home and usage really ramps up. This is entirely unacceptable to me. I don’t mind its limited use as one of many tools in school, but I think inviting screens in to a greater extent opens up all of the issues above, plus the potential for cyber bullying.

I am a Pediatric Speech Language Pathologist and the concerns about kids being on screens is so large, it is so detrimental to the growth of children's communication skills - social languge, expressive langauge, receptive language, attention, focus, physical growth, etc.

Yes, I had a meeting with the district recently about inappropriate content that showed up in an innocent search by my son.  The general response was that 'google doesn't know how this happened'. It did not make me feel good that the district is relying on some outside company to keep these devices safe.

I would lock down more things but it is needed for school. Chromebook’s have been a huge distraction for my oldest with Executive Functioning needs. The students don’t know how to write well, can’t spell. Don’t know how to use an index in a textbook, etc.

Parents cannot see how many minutes per day students are on school-provided Chromebooks while at school. We can use the Securly app to see what websites are used and at what time students log on to these websites, but we cannot see when they log off.
I think it’s bad that the school uses so much you tube and the kids get access to you tube shorts. It’s addictive and completely unnecessary and undermines that we try to limit screen time at home.

It is not readily available to see how much time our children are actually on screens during the school day. The school uses Securly, which parents have access to. It show what websites your child accesses, and when they get on to that website, but not when they leave it.

Every assignment is on the Chromebook. My child can barely write out a math problem. It’s extremely disheartening to think of my children’s future and their inability to write a simple birthday card because their handwriting has been hijacked.
There needs to be more hands on learning, especially with more focus on writing.

Access to content for purchase and YouTube type platform. 
Must be brought home despite home technology.

Phoenixville Area.text: I would like screens banned in elementary school ideally but at minimum a return to shared bank that they visit on a rotation. I think it's terrible that my kids use screens more than they have gym in a week.  We can learn typing and concepts of coding etc without  damaging devices and gym should be every day.

There is little to no research that states any benefits for kids being on technology, especially as it relates to their learning. It’s also difficult to actually monitor what kids are doing and how, even with filters and such.

Get rid of FID days, bring back snow days. School tells students they can use their iPad at home on their own, but that is in direct conflict with our family policy (no personal devices ever; kids are currently 3 and 6yo).
Recent research showing trend of education decline with increased screens in the classroom.
There is nothing a 5 year old is learning that requires an iPad. It is the only reason I have considered private school.

Pittsburgh.text: All of it. The schools that are implementing this stuff are not capable of keeping up with technology and technology use, and are requiring children to be better than adaults by not being distracted or avoiding their real work. Like they want to complain about the kids behavior, but pps created this problem.

I am concerned about required usage for "educational" apps that school districts make for our students, which then require them to use apps a certain amount regardless of their effectiveness. Selling our students' screen time to app developers and technology companies is completely inappropriate.

ALL OF THESE THINGS (for my middle schooler). I have started doing his dreamboxes for him because I cannot bear him being on a screen any more than he already is (all day). Even his old school English teacher is having them do work and writing on the computer all day.

School seems uninterested in intervening in constant notifications and distractions in MS Teams as children use the chat feature. School also uses YouTube for educational content and thus my children spend hours staring at YouTube shorts on their school devices.

There's nothing to prevent kids from watching YouTube or playing video games when they should be listening to the teacher, doing schoolwork, or collaborating with classmates. At home, my kids have the computers in their bedrooms. They could be goofing off on the computer rather than doing work, or they could be using it when they should be sleeping.

My kids' school district will implement a bell-to-bell policy for the 2026-2027 school year, but each school decides how to implement it. I fear that this will enable the entire policy to be undermined to the extent that it won't result in the positive impacts we want for the kids.

Kids are required to use devices in class, then both punished and fall behind in work when the devices prove to be distracting. They are required to use them for math, science, and other subjects, but receive pushback when they ask to use the computers to take notes.

Would also prefer less TV at school. Majority of the time they seem to go to the auditorium to watch TV or movies for recess instead of going outside, even when when it isn't especially cold. So many TV /YouTube brain breaks. So many random days where they're watching a movie for no apparent reason.

My kids use 5+ types of software products for their education. Each one has its own technical challenges, bugs, poor functionality and idiosyncrasies that make using them difficult and detracts from the actual educational value. It seems like the districts answer to everything is a new software product.

As a parent and a teacher in Pittsburgh Public Schools, I have repeatedly contacted the district with concerns that there is no filtering software, like Go Guardian. The students are able to watch Instagram, YouTube, go on Microsoft Teams in every single class, etc. It is so disruptive to learning and teachers are powerless to stop it.

Particularly for math, children are less likely to “work”out a problem in the computer than they might be on a piece paper and for many it just becomes easier to “guess” than try to use an electronic scratch pad to solve a problem.

Studies have also shown that when a person hand writes (rather than typing) that visual memory is enhanced about a topic.

We all know that screen time is not really very good for our kids. Why are we using it in schools for almost every assignment. The trend should be away from screen use, and back to analog. IEP‘s 504s should include the possibility of paper assignments for kids with ADHD and other learning issues.

We raised our children to play with other humans and create things with their hands, and they were quite happy doing all of these things and being regular socialized children until PPS issued and required children to use these devices and bring them to and from school like little businesspeople.

I work as a volunteer in the school and the moment the kids get a break, they are immediately on a screen. Their retention is horrific and they do not know to look up and communicate unless I’m yelling for their attention.

As mentioned above, I find it egregious that students are testing on laptops just to prepare for PSSAs (a state-mandated standardized test to keep the Commonwealth in compliance with No Child Left Behind, all of which I find to be questionable in merit and purpose).

We have asked our first-grader's teacher to allow her to not bring her device home at night as we do not want her playing on the computer on the bus or after school and undermining our home rules against screen time. But this puts her behind on homework assignments and then unable to participate on remote learning days

Students are required to bring home their computers every night to charge them. Most students walk to school and their backpacks are extremely heavy because of this. Some students have even gotten backpacks on wheels because they are so heavy.
I wish my school district would share what sort of policy they have for how they use screen time and how much of the school day is spent on a screen.

My sixth grader reports that he and the other students use their laptops during recess and all other breaks in time that used to be filled with book reading, physical activity, and social interaction.
I really think the computers should stay at home and be used for remote learning or homework only. I don’t want my child ever using a device at school unless it is in the library on a shared device.
My preference would be no screens whatsoever in grades PreK-5, some very intentional introduction to technology for learning in Middle School, and then increased use in High School.
I’m deeply concerned about a lack of digital literacy skills being addressed as well as a reliance on AI. It’s a bad combo.
My daughter spends way too much time on screens at school. Much of her curriculum is screen based. She's exhausted and unregulated when she gets home.
"Education" programs used by the district have minimal educational value, and teach repeated guessing rather than deep engagement with content.
We are highly concerned about the ed tech; it is being used to supplement short staff.
I believe we should follow in the footsteps of Sweden and do away with them; as test scores have overall gone down.
Remote learning days requiring 7 hours a day on a computer that barely supports the technology
Definitely wish that “fun” screen use at school was replaced with outdoor time and physical activity.
My child sometimes doesn’t know what she’s doing when she goes through the activities.

Wallingford-Swarthmore.text: In one of my kids classes the teacher doesn't lecture, he tells them to move through the screen by screen learning modules on the chrome books in the classroom. I feel strongly that this is removing the opportunity for kids to listen to a lecture. This is very sad to me. I am also very upset about the constant every day, all day, updates of their grades.

We need all devices out of our schools, save for a few select cases. The research is clear that all devices (laptops, phones, etc.) hinder student learning, focus, and engagement. Technology also represents a large spend, which is an issue in out district.

My son talks about videos he’s watching at school about Minecraft and also says he has access to YouTube.  These platforms are inappropriate for school.  This is ridiculous and unacceptable.  The teachers default to screens way way too often.  If the teachers need a break, give the kids more recess time, not screen time.

The district’s reliance on devices is misguided. Routine screen use is not synonymous with academic rigor or future readiness. Students do not need constant exposure to technology to succeed in a digital world — they need strong literacy, critical thinking, attention, and interpersonal skills.

Kids have plenty of opportunity to interact with screens outside of school, and I hate to see kids dedicating to screens the school hours that could be spent interacting with peers and staff, creating hands on work, extra recess, or diving into extracurricular topics within the classroom.

I'm hearing from my kids that they are using their chromebooks during indoor recess and various times throughout the school day to play games. I don't really mind chromebooks used for instructional purposes starting in middle school (not elementary), but I would prefer that they not be the go-to for indoor recess or any downtime throughout the day.

I hate that she’s on screens, for even things like math games.  She should be on paper and pencil.  This is be you retain facts by writing it.  I know this from my own studying  in nursing school of how to retain hard facts.   This is going to be a real problem.  I want to push back and enforce paper and pencil.

I've worked hard to keep screens (specifically ipads, youtube) out of my children's life at home and they are being exposed to it at school of all places. I was not given an opt in/out form and it is unclear how often devices are being used. And I don't know what apps/games/tools are being provided or offered.

I would like my child's teacher to provide more specific information about how and when they use the chromebook in the classroom.  I'm not too concerned about how much it is used based on the information I receive from my child.

My school does not restrict access to screens during critical socializing periods (transition between class, lunch) when kids can actually talk to each other; they are going to the bags, getting the phones, and "catching up" rather than actually socializing with one another face-to-face and developing those skills

Everything is dependent upon this technology. If the chromebook is not working - all work stops. There is no back-up! Teachers are heavily reliant upon playing videos in school to teach. That is not engaging to children, it is not how their brains learn.
My MS kids spend all day on their chrome books and come home and do a majority of their homework on it as well.  There is no break.  No actual writing being done.
I asked administrators about opting out of Chromebook’s usage for our sixth grader and it definitely felt like it was the first time anyone had ever asked and they had no prepared solution in place.
Too much focus on screen "games" especially during indoor recess when time could be spent socializing, real games, arts or crafts.
When our now 3rd grader was in 1st grade, we had some concerns that it felt like they were constantly on their ipads in school.
My son likely has ADHD and uses a behavior chart at school to help with impulse control, but the reward is sometimes screen time which seems counterintuitive
I think it's too easy for teachers to assign a video teaching something rather than teaching the content themselves.
I would appreciate the teacher sharing at the beginning of the year how tech is used in the classroom.
Considering our districts budget concerns, having devices at this age of student feels unnecessary.

Mt. Lebanon.text: My daughter really dislikes when everyone is on their screen during recess. Thankfully this has decreased with new rules. I am concerned about whether generative AI tools will be used as I do not trust them to always be accurate or appropriate. I wish we would have whitelisted sites instead of blacklisting some sites as there will always be new websites.

I don't think there should be 1:1 devices for k-8. Kids need to develop handwriting and spelling skills. They need to think for themselves instead of having Google (and now AI) at their fingertips. They need to learn without computer games. Filters don't work; there are constantly new unblocked gaming sites popping up.

My kids have been able to sneak access to inappropriate content on YouTube after a friend showed them how to use Siri to access it when the teacher was helping another child. Teachers don’t want this access, and should be able to block websites, but they do not districts hasn’t given them teacher access to go guardian.

My sons preschool/prek does not include devices, but kindergarten will and I am extremely unhappy about this and hoping to change it before he enters school with our local unplugged group. We do not allow devices at home (our child does not have a device and does not have permission to use ours) and we limit and are selective with tv, movies etc.

The Chromebooks are the bane of my existence. The kids are not learning correctly. The screens have infiltrated every aspect of our lives and we need to return to paper and pencil so that the children can actually retain what they are supposed to be learning.

I think my child has not learned executive functioning skills because he has not learned how to write down assignments due and organize his homework and study time because everything is on Schoology instead of a paper calendar.  We started doing this ourselves but a lot to catch up on.

There is a lack of fundamental explanation on the districts edtech use including what they use, why they use it, and what the expectations are for the students.  When asked for these details there appears to be a purposeful layer of obfuscation which is especially concerning.

I don’t like how open search is, even something as simple as searching for images to change their backgrounds. I think digital book reports are a cop out. My son’s handwriting is bad and I think they should write more.

I understand the use of computers during self directed times like when teachers have small groups. I don’t see why it is necessary during whole class instruction time like in Spanish or music classes.
My 5th grader relies too heavily on his school issued Chromebook at home. It’s such a distraction and most of his work isn’t completed on a screen.
Pop up ads for pay to play "academic" games and having access to YouTube- even if "shorts" are disabled and content restricted, we feel youtube is terrible for kids at a young age.
My son is in preschool but I am aware of and anticipating issues with the tech policies at school starting in kindergarten based on information shared with me from other parents in the district
Math class is almost entirely computer game based, which has been ineffective

Quaker Valley.text: Have had multiple instances with both children being exposed to inappropriate/ mature content on their school devices, despite school filters. Both of my children have ADHD and screens exacerbate that. My oldest also uses their school device as an 'escape' during class when they are overwhelmed.

For kids with learning disabilities devices can create larger hurdles as expectations assignments, etc. become more ambiguous and the distraction of playing games and streaming videos is at their fingertips and they do not have the impulse control to stay focused on school tasks and assignments.

In schools that take home devices, I worry about the kids whose parents are not home or not monitoring screen time and kids get addicted to devices and exposed to inappropriate content at young ages sets them up for failure and makes breaking addictions much harder because of the early exposure.

I have been at the library before to volunteer and the kids are on iPads.. this makes 0 sense to me. Another thing is my children talk about googling our family.... and seeing pictures of things. I think this is super inappropriate  to do. Children should not have access to to Google.. they are in 1st and 2nd and 3rd grade searching on google.

Just reiterating the impact of everything I’ve checked above and below. We have a major over use on our school iPads and it’s made it very hard to control/monitor/limit in the higher grade levels. We are seeing the negative effects of this in our 6th grader. Everything listed here in this survey is critical and needs to be done.

Technology should be used for research and final draft writing. The students have the devices in their hands all day and watch YouTube and play “unblocked” games when they should be socializing or getting their work done for class.

In higher grades (but still elementary school), I’ve heard of AI introduction. I’m appalled. Ai has no place in elementary school. Elementary school is to teach students how to do the research, the math, the grammar, etc on their own. Not how to have ai do it for them.

Our kids definitely need to learn and use technology- in a way that we probably can’t even understand yet. But that said- limiting use is very important and I want them to be used as a tool to aide in learning and developing and not AS the instruction. ex.

The amount of substitution being done at the elementary level is very concerning. My children are being asked to use the device for schoolwork before they ever actually did the equivalent work with paper and pencil. I also think our district uses the devices in an "educational way" to keep kids quiet.

One concern I have is that my child attends an after school program with kids in older grades who have access to electronic devices whether they are through school or parents allow them to bring them. The screen time does not appear to be regulated and it makes me uncomfortable with the types of content he might be exposed to.

I’m concerned that schools have adopted tech without asking first why use of the tech is a better tool to teach kids certain subjects. Said another way, where’s the data that this tool (iPad, app, etc.) results in better learning outcomes?

Schools are not using evidence based practices and best practices for learning. They should be reading the studies of how students learn and it is not 2 dimensional with screens. It is multi sensory with writing and reading paper.

Though screens can be used for educational purposes, kids don't see it that way. They see it as a reward and it's inappropriate. Additionally, I have seen many kids easily subvert the lockdown and access inappropriate content.

I worry about the amount of work done on the iPads, especially screen-to-screen work, meaning looking up information on their iPad and recording it on their iPad. I have seen the impact on my son that having study materials on the screen have done. His grades were slipping.

Research shows students do not learn as well from screens as they do from physical text books and paper-pencil activities. Physically writing something down also helps one retain that information better than typing it.

She is missing out on the fine motor skills and kinetic learning from writing. It's hard for me to check her work or help her without a workbook or text book. I am concerned about the security of the filters.

My son had been watching videos about pro wrestlers on his device at school during library time instead of reading. It seems like too often the screen time at school serves little academic purpose that couldn't be better filled by traditional teaching.

Devices have taken away traditional methods of learning.  All text books are electronic.  All notes are completed  via the iPad.  Students don’t learn good study habits and it’s hard to go back to the text books to complete homework assignments when they are both on the same device

Decreased retention of what is read, decreased transference of skills practice on iPad to other situations, decreased executive functioning.  The kids are getting a worse education compare to using physical books, pen and paper, etc.
The ability of my child to focus on school lessons while having access to a device.  We had to trake his applewatch away at home because even that was too distracting
After 9:00 PM, my children's phones disconnect automatically, and they no longer have access; however, they have access to their tablets at all times, as they need to do their homework on them.
My son cannot spell, and I was literally told by teachers and staff that it doesn’t matter because they have spellcheck.
These kids are incapable of reading, writing and communicating because of these devices.
I would like to know what the value is in the programs they are using is.

Wissahickon.text: Wissahickon SD ended its 1:1 device policy for K & 1st in the fall of 2024. My older child watched entire episodes of PBS Kids cartoons or played games every day all through kindergarten. Eliminating the 1:1 policy made such a difference for my younger child's experience.

I am very concerned that I don't know how much screen time they get every day, but my 5th grader says it's a lot. I do not like that it's used as a reward or break-- I would much rather my children get to move around at recess and other times during the day.

I do not use screens at home except for occasional tv or movie night. My kids are constantly asking for brain breaks or YouTube because they watched it at school. These are designed to “hook” children with their fast pace and bright colors and they get nothing out of it (particularly if it’s not a movement one).

I have two neurodivergent children who become hyper focused with the computer time. They would choose to be on the computers over recess and they become zoned in and oblivious to everything else around them. I also have concerns that things are not locked down appropriately and they have access to YouTube and other sites we don’t let them watch at home.

I am anxious to see how my concerns change as my daughter enters middle school next year. 

I would also like to see a research based digital wellness curriculum and more transparency from the district regarding device use.

I'd rather them focus on presentation skills, keyboard skills if they are going to use the computers. I do know they can go to YouTube and not sure what content is filtered or not.  They also do not share with us how much screentime each kid gets a week.

Teachers seem to use unpaid youtube all the time, and kids are FREQUENTLY exposed to ads. 
Some teachers use screens more than others - many as the default for teaching concepts. My kids will talk about a topic (science, history etc), and when I ask more about it, way too frequently it seems to be from a video.

-Teachers seem to use unpaid youtube all the time, and kids are FREQUENTLY exposed to ads. 
-Some teachers use screens more than others - many as the default for teaching concepts. My kids will talk about a topic (science, history etc), and when I ask more about it, way too frequently it seems to be from a video.
Too slow to act on current research demonstrating a broad range of harms related to ed tech. Other districts are more nimble when it comes to research to practice implementation.

Generally concerned about controls on access to games, YouTube, and the chat. I feel like kids are learning racist and mysogynist language at an earlier age that I remember. I would also like to see them have a bit of distance from the news
There is no reason children should have screens at school. Test scores are in free fall and the district refuses to consider that tech overuse is partly why.
I worry that computer literacy is taking precedence over actual intelligence and literacy. Kids barely have work any more or know how to write essays.
Everything is gamefied. Traditional learning without reward breaks now seems mundane

Greater Nanticoke Area.text: We were not given an option of wanting a device. We are also responsible for any damages that may occur to said device throughout the year. Students also bring device home throughout the summer further undermining our strict no device policy at home.

Abington Heights.text: My son’s teacher told us that there is no way for the school to block everything inappropriate. As parents, I feel as if the school is undoing a lot of our hard work when it comes to limiting screen time and ensuring we know what they are accessing.

School District of Philadelphia.text: The children are always trying to figure out ways to "jailbreak" their chromebooks to access restricted sites, or just finding new sites that aren't restricted yet. My child doesn't use the school-issued chromebook at home, but has to carry it around to and from school everyday because there is "no place to store it".

The School District of Philadelphia issue chromebooks to families during COVID but then kept them in place afterwards without asking families if we wanted to take that on. We're now responsible for these devices and we don't want to be. Additionally, the protections aren't great and parents can't put controls on them.

I'm deeply against the use of generative AI in schools in any grade. I support the consideration of and research into the benefits of analytical AI for teachers, for certain projects, etc, but think generative AI use runs counter to the types of cognitive skills I most want my kids to develop.

Screen time replaces teacher led interactions. For example, the teacher doesn't do story time on the rug in my child's Kindergarten class. Instead, a book is played on YouTube on the smart board instead. Although both are stories, I feel like the YouTube story is unnecessary.
We are never told what the screen policy is or how much they are using. I’m reliant on my 7th to tell me how she played “x” math game all afternoon

My biggest concern is school devices undermining screen time policies at home. My 5th grader exclusively has homework on her Chromebook; it’s difficult to police where homework ends and YouTube time begins.
EdTech is encouraging disengagement in learning particularly among teens.
For my younger one, I also don't like how much assessment is done on a computer with no adult present

West Chester Area.text: I am disappointed that writing assignments are being completed on an ipad from start to finish.  I think they are missing an important learning experience from editing their paper and each other's papers before typing a final draft, since they are unable to print a rough draft to look over.

In our three years of 1:1 devices, I have not seen one project or use of the technology that has impressed me. I also think it's important to note how these devices are being used to "show" work back to parents, sometimes the kids themselves are uploading and sharing their work. It just feels like busy work to do all this sharing.

Data privacy, reinforcing addictive product design (gamification), location tracking, and normalization of personal device use at home at way too young of an age. There needs to be an opt-out option for elementary school at a minimum.
Too much of a distraction for them to focus on the assignment vs going to other apps to play games.
Besides the overuse of iPads, I strongly despise how teachers are getting away from traditional read alouds, and instead, turning to YouTube videos of other people reading a book to them.
In three years of public school, I have yet to see a positive benefit of using iPads in the classroom in the early elementary school years.

State College Area.text: We are a family with very limited screen time at home. I am not pleased that the State College Area School District, a district in a highly-educated area surrounded with research PhDs, allows so much screen time in the schools.

I do not think that kindergarteners should have computers or personal screen time AT ALL. Starting later (even third or fourth grade) would be better. I have looked at the "educational" programs my children use at school, and most, especially the reading apps, have no educational value at all in my opinion.

Spring-Ford Area.text: It's amazing to me that despite a mountain of data memorializing the brain damage caused by screens, and showing that screen-based "educational" apps are counterproductive, that we are stoop surveying or debating this. Should we debate cigarette smoking in elementary school?

Rose Tree Media.text: It’s generally unclear how much access to technology the kids have. We try to compensate by limiting screens at home and will wait till at least 8th grade for cell phone. But it feels like the access to technology at school undermines our technology choices.

I think smart phones are here to stay. They’re an integral part of everybody’s day-to-day life, however with the rise of AI, the ability to critically think and to be able to deeply reconsider or consider the output of artificial intelligence is going to be an increasingly rare and important skill if we build a generation of children that depend on…
We currently have no way to know how much screen time the kids have while they're in school so I can't say if it's too much or not.
When school was using iPads my kids would take them to art class to use when finished with their work. I don’t understand why they can’t use actual paper and pencils for free drawing time
I don't have knowledge of how often my student is on the school device, what activities/apps he has access to etc.

Jenkintown.text: I saw my son write an email and right click to correct the highlighted errors. But didn’t understand what the errors actually were or how to fix them without the email aid. It’s frustrating because I raised this exact issue years ago to the language arts teacher. It’s like nobody thinks this is a problem?
My daughter was able to access an inappropriate website with a chat feature. When I alerted the school they fixed it and said their protection software had expired and they forgot to renew it…
The kids have figured out how to bypass the weak guardrails and watch YouTube and have weird AI conversations on a device that is essentially not supervised by anyone.
My main concern is that I have yet to see evidence that learning on the device surpasses regular analog methods.
Teachers are using chromebooks to keep kids quiet and the looking the other way while the children play games.
Chromebooks being used as babysitters during school. Students allowed to play games too often.

Upper Merion Area.text: Students are allowed to use devices during indoor recess and breaks. I’d prefer that they use that time to socialize play board games/card games, etc. I also feel iPads become used as a classroom management tool to keep kids quiet and behaved rather than allowing them other options to do when they are done, waiting, etc.

Ridley.text: My child has ADHD and it's too easy for him to look like he's working when he's actually playing browser-based games. He also struggles to remember to turn in assignments through online class management systems because the work is done on paper, but he's still required to take photos of his work to submit.

My daughter says teachers don't teach, they just say "go to [insert app] on ipad, watch the video and answer the questions." She says as a result of this, teachers don't know how to teach the material they've been tasked with teaching when kids have a question.

Fox Chapel Area.text: Strongly dislike that they have "free time" during the day where they are allowed to just be on their iPads unrestricted (YouTube kids, violent games with ads, etc.). This is indoor lunch, breaks, and waiting for the bus, so it adds up to well more than our recommended 30-60 minutes per day, especially since they can be on literally any site.

There is not enough tactile learning for learning retention 

Tech will change in 10-15 years, they don’t need this access right now 

The most unsupervised screen time my son gets is at school 

No clear direction on filters etc. 

Who is profiting from this?

All the latest peer-reviewed data shows that the more time spent on screens, including smart boards, the less quality learning takes place. 
Screens also effext emotional regulation and denigrate parent-student interactions.

Jersey Shore Area.text:
The older the grade the more they're seen on their devices.

I also believe that Keyboarding should be more of a priority in the elementary grades. If students are exposed/required to become so dependent on using the technology, why are they be supported in how to use it appropriately and develop a lifelong skill.

Platforms to access coursework is cumbersome and frustrating to some students; remote learning days are worksheets in digital format…no interaction with teacher or immediate feedback to check for understanding. Students are connected to a device to cover the material in many classes when weather days prevent them from attending in person.

I would like more information communicated to families about the actual use of the iPads during school, such as the apps and programs that are being used, the average amount of screen time per day, etc.
Just what’s already been said. We need to get back to using books and putting pencil to paper. Have a classes with allotted time in a computer lab if technology is needed.
Early language is essential to learning to read. Parents and students do not communicate anymore. Families "sit" on their devices. The addiction is real
It’s more difficult for me, as a parent, to know and see what my child is learning, and it is more difficult for me to help him study for tests.

Pennridge.text: It's impossible for the teachers to truly monitor what the students are all doing on their computers, especially in the older grades.  I have heard of many instances where students are supposed to be working on an assignment at their desks and instead they are playing games, google searching, watching YouTube, or just surfing the web.

West Shore.text: I genuinely have no idea how much time my daughter is in her iPad every day. The school doesn’t communicate it well. We used to do screens on a regular basis at home but we have stopped because of how much ambiguity there is with her school usage.

Avonworth.text: Since 3rd grade brings theirs home (without any homework assigned at home...seems like it is just in case of snow day but still every night) they have wifi access on the bus while they wait to leave or in the morning before school starts. My son tells me they Google things or play computer games.
Devices should NOT be an option during indoor recess in my opinion. Recess should be a protected screen free time for open play.

Upper Darby.text: I am very concerned about the use of AI, privacy and that learning and testing is done solely on the computers. Kids are not given any proper preparation to use the computers. The schools freely hand them out without any guidance whatsoever.

Norwin.text: I absolutely hate screens and the negative effects it has on my ADHD child. I wish there was an option to opt out.  Science shows how detrimental screens are to children yet we continue to rely on them.  It makes nonsense.  It is unfair to the families who are trying to be screen free.
These iPad usages weather or not they use a little or a lot is just too much. They are causing more issues with learning and social factors. I would rather my child use pencil and paper.

My 6 year old son in kindergarten told me today that “most of his friends watch YouTube on the school iPad” 
6… years… old… in kindergarten.

Lower Moreland Township.text: My daughter only ever talks about the games her and her friends play on their devices. No amount of filters will bypass kids' ability to find unproductive ways to use these devices. I'd rather the school have a computer lab where kids have a dedicated class to learn about technology.
I am on the school's wellness committee  and with another mom trying to for a Wait Until 8th/ digital Balance page. My 4th grader is on Chromebook entire day including indoor recess.
Kids are not learning to read or write. Spell check is taking over all writing so my 6th graders cannot spell basic works using pen and paper

Abington.text: It has been proven many times over that children learn best by real experiences. Project based learning is the best approach for the education of children, but the education system in this country was not created by educators so it’s doing a severe injustice to our children.

Unionville-Chadds Ford.text: In particular, attention span, critical thinking, writing.  I'd prefer pencil, paper, textbooks.  100%.  Type the final version, some online research would be okay with me.

Radnor Township.text: While the filters are good, there's still some content that they do not block out, particularly some websites such as youtube.  We're not a youtube household and as such where quite surprise that most teachers use it for the students during the day sometimes.

Eastern Lancaster County.text: Our school district allows YouTube, which is not something our children have access to at home. Our district also uses Aristotle, however it works haphazardly. This past fall, my 12 year old came across and Ai chat site through his school issued Chromebook.

South Fayette Township.text: In general, I believe that school issued devices are a good thing. However, not enough time is spent limiting the amount of time. Students are using those devices at school and that carries over to home.

Beaver Area.text: Most of our major conflicts in our home with our younger kids have been around ipad use at home and on the bus. In fact, I've begun to take my 3rd schooler to and from school just to alleviate 30 minutes each way of extra ipad use on games, youtube, etc.
I am afraid of how the issues listed above are overall harming my child's cognitive development.

Pequea Valley.text: My younger children have never even used an iPad or tablet or computer, then they’re expected to use devices at 5-6 years old. Let them be kids and explore the world through senses, not screens.

North Penn.text: We find the chromebooks to be a very negative experience. Teachers assign homework to our 9 and 11 year old that leads to even MORE screen time after being on the devices throughout the day. I would prefer no screens in elementary school.
Concerned about correlation between increased screen time at schools and general decline in learning and test score statewide and nationally

Avon Grove.text: I myself grew up with a public school issued laptop and it created more problems than solved. I was already technologically savvy and it was an unnecessary distraction from school.

Cheltenham Township.text: We don’t allow YouTube in our home but the kids can access it on their school Chromebooks. Same for general search engines like Google. And my 6th grader has one teacher who allows kids to go on their phones during free time.
Too much access to Google and other Ai options to solve problems.  Less student interaction,  independent thought, and ability to look at text and independent writing at the same time.
The kids have enough screens in their lives in general and the use of them at school to the degree they are utilized is frustrating and worrisome.
My 5th grader thinks she needs to be able to do math in her head vs paper because of the way math is taught, iXL…

Downingtown Area.text: Our district requires us to pay for device insurance. Even if we push back to leave devices at school they still say it’s mandatory Id pay $1,800 for my 3 children to go through k-12. To be forced to use something that hinders their growth- it’s absurd
The strain and physical impact on the children's bodies, especially elementary kids, carrying Chromebooks back and forth from school every day.
Both my children have to transport their chromebooks back and forth to school everyday, making their backpacks too heavy for little kids to carry.

Wyomissing Area.text: It's difficult to help them study when everything is electronic. I never see them read books and don't think electronic texts help them learn with depth.

Tredyffrin-Easttown.text: Screen time during “free time” means kids play on apps instead of interacting with each other. Some teachers use screen time as a reward. Use of blacklist instead of whitelist**** this is a major issue.

When laptops are used as a digital worksheet instead of a critical thinking tool it defeats the purpose of using the device at all.  David  Jonassen's
"Computers as Mindtools for Schools: Engaging Critical Thinking"
I'm surprised to hear how most of my 6th grader's class time is spent in front of a laptop.

Neshaminy.text: There needs to be a different approach with younger children who dont know how to read or use a computer. What to click for pop ups, terms & conditions, location tracking, personalized ads, etc.

Springfield.text:
We use Bark for our kids’ smart phones and other devices within the home
Connected to Internet. School district does not permit such parental oversight and has weak restrictions on home WiFi.

Corry Area.text: Data privacy is my only concern. Use of third-party services are so tempting for schools that do not have the budgets or skills to build the technology they want to use or teach in-house.

Fairview.text: I'm working with a group of moms to get a bell to bell ban at the high school. It's progressing but it's already behind others.

Stroudsburg Area.text: Children are not taught how to read/write/type before being given chromebooks to use for school assignments.   Kindergarteners should not have Chromebooks at all!

Antietam.text: Research shows that information is better retained when students used physical books and worksheets.

North East.text: I think that there is such an emphasis on screens rather than hands on learning. It is concerning the eye strain it gives children as well.

Jim Thorpe Area.text: Ignorance of how screens impact learning (teachers not given a choice and also not provided information on how to utilize technology without negatively impacting learning)

Governor Mifflin.text: I am a substitute teacher in my child's school district and have seen firsthand how impossible it is to get kids to stay on task when 1:1 devices are used

Council Rock.text: My district has no guidelines for usage for teachers and as a result, my kids are on it too much.

Palmyra Area.text: Some grade levels are allowed to have their screens at lunch, which is of major concern to me.

Parkland.text: Chromebooks should stay in School Carts during the week and taken out when needed.

Pine-Richland.text: i wish there was a conversation about this in our community but there isn't (yet).

Souderton Area.text: Screens are used far too much and for non academic enhancing purposes.

Haverford Township.text: Everything is gamified. It’s not good for developing internal motivation,
